text: The surgeon creates a large flap across the surgical field, revealing a lower midline diastasis. The flap is dissected fully across without addressing the diastasis, and meshes are placed without re-approximating the rectus muscles.
The surgeon dissects medially towards Cooper's ligament, carefully navigating around the large sliding hernias.
The surgeon utilizes three ports for the procedure, with an additional five-millimeter port placed on the right side for retraction assistance. The appendix is visible during the operation.

The surgeon has exposed the hernia sac, revealing the sliding component of the bowel. The clearer visibility allows for more confident dissection as the surgeon searches for the chord structures, which are not yet visible.

The surgeon receives assistance for finer retraction and dissection, revealing the cord structures on the lower midline of the screen. The cord is visibly pulled up onto the hernia sac as it is being extracted.

The surgeon carefully reduces the hernia sac, pulling the cord structures over while dissecting. The appendix is visible as it is pulled back into the field. The surgeon ensures there is no bowel within the sac before proceeding to thin out the tissue at the distal extent of the hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens adjacent to the hernia sac, providing traction to improve visibility. The appendix is visible again within the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the edge of the hernia sac, having completed the right side and checked for a cord lipoma, which was absent. The focus shifts to the left side, revealing a sliding indirect hernia involving the sigmoid colon, with a visible direct component of pericolic fat.

The surgeon carefully dissects the cord structures laterally, lifting them with assistance to separate them from the lateral aspect of the hernia sac. The dissection follows a clear plane down into the inguinal canal to fully reduce the sliding hernia.

The surgeon completes the dissection by hugging the hernia sac, with the vas deferens visible. After confirming the absence of a cord lipoma on the left side, the surgeon prepares to place two 16x12 anatomic ProGrip meshes.
The surgeon secures two anatomical pro grip meshes to each other, as well as to Cooper's ligament and the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring stability despite the presence of diastasis.
The surgeon has placed two meshes in position and proceeds to close the peritoneal flap. The closure begins in the middle of the flap, with suturing extending to one side and then the other.
The surgeon closes the right side of the peritoneal flap after completing the closure on the left side, ensuring the flap is securely sutured in place.
After undocking, the surgeon places an angiocath into the surgical space and connects it to suction, effectively evacuating the area.
